data.title: Sanofi gets EU nod for Sarclisa combination in multiple myeloma
data.description:
• Sanofi (NASDAQ:SNY) announced that the European Commission has approved Sarclisa to be used alongside bortezomib, lenalidomide, and dexamethasone for multiple myeloma treatment.
  • This approval is especially intended for the initial treatment of adult patients who have just been diagnosed with multiple myeloma and are suitable for a stem cell transplant.
  • The  approval comes after results from the phase 3 study showed that using Sarclisa combination significantly improved the rate of minimal residual disease negativity and extended progression-free survival.

data.title: Hims & Hers drops as U.S. lawmakers target copycat weight loss drugs
data.description:
Shares of Hims & Hers Health (NYSE:HIMS) reached a session low on Friday after a bipartisan group of U.S. lawmakers urged the Food and Drug Administration (FDA) to act against copycat versions of weight loss drugs, citing safety concerns.

In a letter sent to the FDA on Friday, more than 80 lawmakers call on the regulator to crack down on fake and knockoff versions of weight loss drugs such as Eli Lilly’s (NYSE:LLY) Zepbound and Novo Nordisk’s (NVO) Wegovy.

“We are concerned about recent reports revealing a surge in illegal and counterfeit anti-obesity medications,” the lawmakers, led by Representatives Richard Hudson of North Carolina and Herb Conaway of New Jersey, wrote in the letter to FDA Commissioner Marty Makary.

“Undoubtedly, illegal counterfeit medications pose an increased risk to patient safety with sometimes fatal consequences.”

Hims & Hers Health (NYSE:HIMS) continues to market compounded versions of Wegovy at a sharp discount to the branded product, even after a years-long shortage of the popular weight loss drug ended early this year.

data.title: EM equities are the most overbought global assets, USD the most oversold – BofA
data.description:
Emerging markets equities (NYSEARCA:EEM) are the most overbought assets in the world, ranked from their 200-day moving average deviation in U.S. dollar terms.

Emerging markets stocks (NYSEARCA:EEM) are up 13.7% from their 2000-day moving average, according to BofA Securities.

In addition, BofA’s “Bull & Bear” Indicator has risen to 6.4 from 6.3, swinging towards the bullish/sell side but still neutral – the highest rise since the November U.S. election – on the back of stronger emerging market stocks and bond inflows and fund manager cash levels, “partially offset by bearish S&P hedge fund futures positioning,” said Michael Hartnett, chief investment strategist at BofA.

Emerging market debt has seen $5.7B in inflows in the past 14 weeks, the largest among other global assets as a percentage of float, and the total emerging market equities inflows amounted to $12.8B in inflows year-to-date.

Also, the U.S. dollar (DXY) depreciating further to below 95 “would be confirmation asset allocators need to execute phase II of dollar debasement...up allocation to commodities and emerging markets,” Hartnett said.

The U.S. dollar (DXY) is the most oversold asset in the world, -6.1% from its 200-day moving average.

Lastly, the world policy rate is down from 4.8% to 4.4% in the past 12 months, and is forecasted to fall to 3.9% in the next 12 months, driven by the Federal Reserve and emerging markets central banks.

data.title: Flagstar Financial outlines $1.5B quarterly C&I loan target as holding company merger streamlines costs
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Flagstar Financial, Inc. (FLG) Q2 2025

Management View

  • Joseph M. Otting, President, CEO & Executive Chairman, opened the call stating management is "very pleased with our operating results achieved during the second quarter," noting progress on C&I growth, cost reduction, and credit quality. Otting highlighted "almost $1.9 billion of commitments and $1.2 billion in new loans" in C&I, with plans to continue expanding by adding talent and diversifying the loan portfolio. He announced the planned merger of the holding company into Flagstar Bank, which is intended to "enhance our corporate, legal and regulatory structure," reduce redundant costs, and streamline operations without changes to management or board structure.

  • Otting detailed four management focus areas for 2025: margin expansion, C&I and private bank growth, CRE portfolio management, and credit quality improvement. He emphasized the addition of 46 new bankers in Q2 and a pipeline of $1.2 billion in C&I lending, stating, "new commitments increased 80% compared to the prior quarter...originations increased almost 60%."

  • Lee Matthew Smith, Senior Executive VP & CFO, stated, "our CET1 capital ratio ended the quarter at 12.3%, ranking us as one of the best capitalized regional banks and our adjusted pre-provision pretax net revenue was a positive $9 million, an improvement of plus $32 million from last quarter as we look to return the bank to profitability in the fourth quarter of this year."

Outlook

  • Management refined 2025 guidance, reducing net interest income and NIM by $125 million and 10 basis points, respectively, due to higher loan payoffs, but offsetting $75 million with lower noninterest expense. Adjusted EPS guidance for 2025 is approximately $0.10 lower than previously forecast. In 2026, guidance for net interest income is lower by $100 million, but noninterest expense is also reduced by $100 million, resulting in unchanged adjusted EPS. There is "no change to our '27 guidance."

  • Otting reiterated the focus on C&I loan growth, fee income, operating expense cuts, and reducing criticized assets, projecting continued C&I portfolio growth in Q3 and a diversified balance sheet goal of "1/3 CRE, 1/3 C&I and 1/3 consumer."

Financial Results

  • The company reported a net loss of $0.19 per diluted share for Q2 2025. On an adjusted basis, the net loss available to common stockholders was $0.14 per diluted share, compared to $0.23 in Q1 2025. Adjusted pre-provision revenue was $9 million, improving by $32 million from Q1.

  • CET1 capital ratio increased to 12.3%. Net interest margin (NIM) improved to 1.81% for the quarter, with June’s NIM at 1.88%. High-cost brokered deposits and borrowings were reduced, providing ongoing margin benefit. Noninterest expense decreased $24 million quarter-over-quarter and over $700 million year-over-year.

  • The CRE portfolio saw record par payoffs of approximately $1.5 billion, nearly double Q1, with CRE balances declining $8 billion year-to-date to $39.7 billion. Reserve coverage on the multifamily portfolio stands at 1.68%, with rent-regulated multifamily loans covered at 2.88%.

Q&A

  • Jared Shaw, Barclays, asked about securities purchases and NIM outlook. Smith replied, "We pulled forward $2 billion...we accelerated those securities purchases into Q2 because it maximizes NIM," and the FHLB payoff benefits are included in the NIM forecast for the remainder of the year.

  • Mark Fitzgibbon, Piper Sandler, questioned stock buyback timing. Smith responded, "right now, the focus is on investing the excess capital in growing C&I and other asset classes...if we get to the middle of '26...that may well be something that we need to take a look at."

  • Christopher McGratty, KBW, discussed balance sheet size and payoff acceleration. Smith explained, "at the end of '25, the balance sheet will be about $93.3 billion...about $2.7 billion less than what we thought it would be at the end of last quarter," reflecting higher than expected CRE payoffs.

  • Ebrahim Poonawala, Bank of America, probed the rent-regulated multifamily portfolio. Smith provided extensive detail, emphasizing a $10 billion exposure with 97% occupancy and significant reserves, stating, "we are more than adequately reserved against the portfolio."

  • Several analysts asked about the impact and rationale for the holding company merger. Otting confirmed, "our estimate is this is roughly $15 million that will reduce our costs."

  • Matthew Breese, Stephens, asked about stress test requirements post-merger. Bao Nguyen, Senior EVP, General Counsel & Chief of Staff, stated, "we will be under the threshold. But with our transaction...we won't even be subject to the requirements."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed cautious optimism, focusing on cost reduction, credit risk, and C&I growth, while raising repeated questions about asset size, buybacks, and portfolio risk. The tone was neutral to slightly positive, with some pressing on guidance cuts and CRE risk.

  • Management maintained a confident yet measured tone, emphasizing progress and discipline in cost and credit management. Otting stated, "we continue to accomplish everything we set out to do and make progress on several fronts."

  • Compared to Q1, management’s confidence was more visible during prepared remarks, while Q&A featured more detailed defense of risk mitigation and strategy adjustments.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance was revised downward for 2025 net interest income and NIM, offset by anticipated cost savings; prior quarter guidance had not anticipated such a significant acceleration in CRE payoffs or reduction in asset base.

  • C&I originations and commitments saw sharper sequential growth, and hiring accelerated, with 46 new bankers in Q2 versus 15 in Q1.

  • CRE reduction efforts intensified, with par payoffs nearly doubling from Q1. Reserve coverage and CET1 capital ratio improved further.

  • Analysts maintained focus on CRE risk and asset base, but the tone was less skeptical, reflecting progress on strategic objectives.

Risks and Concerns

  • Challenges highlighted include the pace of CRE and multifamily payoffs, ongoing exposure to criticized and classified loans, and the impact of New York rent regulations. Management cited rigorous reserve reviews and ongoing financial statement analysis.

  • The holding company merger presents some operational change, but management and legal counsel expressed confidence in its benefits and negligible downsides.

  • Analysts probed the risk of further asset base shrinkage and the health of rent-stabilized multifamily loans, as well as the sustainability of margin improvements amid ongoing runoff and deposit repricing.

Final Takeaway

Flagstar Financial’s Q2 2025 call highlighted accelerated execution on C&I growth, aggressive CRE reduction, and cost efficiencies through the planned holding company merger. Management’s guidance adjustments reflect a smaller but more resilient balance sheet, while capital and reserves remain strong. The company reiterated its commitment to return to profitability in Q4 2025 and to transform into a top-performing regional bank, leveraging new talent and a diversified lending focus.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Market Voices: Trump on Fed rates, Canada; Paramount-Skydance
data.description:
Seeking Alpha's roundup of statements, announcements and remarks that could impact markets, sectors or individual stocks.

  • President Trump said he sees Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell recommending that the central bank lower interest rates after meeting with him Thursday.

"I think we had a very good meeting on interest rates. And [Powell] said to me ... very strongly, the country is doing well,’” Trump told reporters, according to CNBC. “I got that to mean that I think he’s going to start recommending lower rates.”

  • Trump also said he's not focused on trade talks with Canada at this time and that existing tariffs may stay in place indefinitely.

“We haven’t really had a lot of luck with Canada,” Trump told reporters on Friday morning, according to Bloomberg.

“I think Canada could be one where they’ll just pay tariffs, not really a negotiation,” Trump added. “We don’t have a deal with Canada. We haven’t been focused on that.”

Currently, most Canadian goods are exempt from new tariffs as they fall under the U.S.-Mexico-Canada trade agreement that was signed under the first Trump administration. The U.S. has, however, hiked tariffs on steel, aluminum and cars imported from Canada, Bloomberg added.

  • The FCC has cleared Skydance's acquisition of Paramount (NASDAQ:PARA) after the companies agreed to "root out the bias" in CBS's programming. 

"Americans no longer trust the legacy national news media to report fully, accurately, and fairly. It is time for a change," FCC Chairman Brendan Carr said in a statement. "That is why I welcome Skydance’s commitment to make significant changes at the once-storied CBS broadcast network."

"In particular, Skydance has made written commitments to ensure that the new company’s programming embodies a diversity of viewpoints from across the political and ideological spectrum. Skydance will also adopt measures that can root out the bias that has undermined trust in the national news media," Carr added.

In addition to CBS, Paramount also owns Comedy Central, BET, Nickelodeon and MTV.

data.title: Scholastic stock soars on optimism for the year ahead; Q4 results beat expectations
data.description:
Shares of educational publisher Scholastic (NASDAQ:SCHL) were up on Friday after its fourth-quarter results beat estimates. The company also provided an upbeat forecast and maintained a positive tone for the year ahead.

SCHL stock surged as much as 25% and is currently the top percentage gainer on the S&P SmallCap 600.

For fiscal year 2026, the company is targeting adjusted EBITDA of $160M to $170M. It expects a 20% growth from last year at the midpoint ($165M) of guidance, mainly driven by disciplined cost management and restructuring initiatives.

It forecast FY26 revenue to grow 2% to 4% during the year. Free cash flow is expected to be in the $30M to $40M range.

"I'm excited about the new fiscal year ahead," CEO Peter Warwick said during the earnings call. "I think we're in good momentum. We've got a positive outlook, and we continue to focus on creating shareholder value and impact; that's so important to us."

For the current quarter, the company expects "a seasonal loss approximately in line with the prior year."

Q4 revenue breakdown: Children's Book Publishing and Distribution $288.2M (+9%); Education Solutions $125.7M (-7%); Entertainment $14.8M; International $76.8M (+8%).

Net income for the quarter fell to $15.4M, or $0.59 per share, from $35.9M, or $1.23 per share, for the same period last year, weighed down by higher cost of goods sold, operating expenses, and provision for income taxes.

On an adjusted per-share basis, the company earned $0.87, beating the consensus estimate of $0.85.

Total revenue was up 7% at $508.3M and ahead of expectations by nearly $14M. 

"Revenue growth was driven primarily by strong trade channel performance across all major markets," CFO Haji Glover said during the earnings call.

data.title: Primis Financial outlines $13M pretax pre-provision earnings target for 2026 amid operating leverage gains
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Primis Financial Corp. (FRST) Q2 2025

Management View

  • Dennis J. Zember, CEO, highlighted "about $8.4 million in net income or $0.34 per share" for the second quarter, noting this included an additional pretax gain of $7.5 million from a portion of the company's interest in PFH, offset by $1.2 million in support for new teams in Primis Mortgage, and a $2 million write-off on maturing promo loans. Zember emphasized that "the #1 thing driving our results right now is very wide operating leverage," with incremental margins in the mid-4% range and operating expenses holding steady or declining. He stated, "We priced about $120 million of deposits in the second quarter and our effective cost was only 2.89%, which is 32% lower than it was the same quarter a year ago." The digital platform raised $36 million at 4.06% and the company reported an 18% annualized growth in checking accounts for the year. Zember noted, "We have negotiated with our core provider a solution that would save us about $300,000 a month starting in August," with additional technology savings to reach $600,000 per month by early 2027.
  • Zember also commented on business lines: the core bank is "almost 70% of our total balance sheet" with a reported ROA of about 1.38% and a very low cost of deposits in the 1.75% range. Primis Mortgage closed $323 million in the quarter, up 52% from the same quarter last year, and Panacea grew to over $500 million in outstanding credit and surpassed $150 million in deposits.
  • Zember stated, "In our comp peer group, we're the fourth cheapest stock. And it's hard to say that as an advantage. But we're the fourth cheapest stock, barely over tangible book value."
  • CFO Matthew Alan Switzer said, "Gross loans held for investment increased almost 12% annualized from March 31 to June 30," led by Panacea and mortgage warehouse. Switzer added, "Noninterest-bearing deposits increased $22 million or 19% annualized in the quarter." He noted, "Core net interest margin, excluding the effects of the consumer program in the second quarter was 3.15% and up from a reported 3.13% last quarter and 280 basis points in the year ago period."
  • Switzer detailed that "excluding interest reversals on consumer program loans in the second quarter, net interest income would have been $27.5 million versus $26.4 million in the first quarter and $24.9 million a year ago."

Outlook

  • Management indicated that incremental margin expansion is expected to continue, with Zember stating, "we can hold this level or lower [OpEx] through the end of '26," and Switzer projecting "run rate pretax pre-provision earnings...expected to grow to over $13 million heading into 2026, which equates to our 1% ROA goal."
  • Zember noted that the company does not expect "any pressures that would cause...earnings pressure or risk issues," and that the trajectory for core noninterest expense is down to $18 million to $18.5 million per quarter in 2026.

Financial Results

  • The quarter included a $7.5 million pretax gain from selling down PFH and net income of $8.4 million or $0.34 per share. Gross loans held for investment increased nearly 12% annualized, and noninterest-bearing deposits grew by $22 million. Core net interest margin was 3.15%.
  • Noninterest income reached $10.6 million, up from $8.5 million last quarter, primarily from mortgage revenue. Provision expense was $1.2 million due to portfolio growth and moderate charge-offs. Normalized core noninterest expense was about $21 million for the quarter.
  • Technology savings and the end of CDI amortization are expected to lower quarterly run rate by $1.5 million, with $900,000 of that expected in Q3.

Q&A

  • Nicholas Thomas Lorenzoni, Stephens Inc.: Asked about loan growth expectations and specifically for Panacea and mortgage warehouse. Zember responded that warehouse could average $250 million to $350 million next year, while Panacea's pipeline could see $100 million to $150 million, but only a portion will hit the balance sheet. Switzer added, "for the rest of this year, maybe low to mid-single digits because mortgage warehouse will moderate in the fourth quarter."
  • Lorenzoni: Asked about net interest margin outlook. Switzer said, "we've been seeing about 2 basis points a month in margin expansion...probably into the mid-3.20s by the time we get to the end of the year."
  • Christopher William Marinac, Janney Montgomery Scott: Asked about deposit and loan growth in the core bank versus digital. Zember stated, "the core bank will outgrow digital on the deposit side," and digital deposit growth is expected to be flat for the rest of the year.
  • Marinac: Inquired about criticized/classified loan improvement and charge-off implications. Zember said, "I don't know that they can get much lower net charge-offs," while Switzer noted, "on a core basis, we really didn't have a high level of charge-offs anyway."
  • Marinac: Asked about expense trajectory. Switzer said, "if we get down to our kind of $18 million to $18.5 million level with the tech savings...from there, we would expect kind of normal inflation, call it, 3% to 4%."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst questions were focused and largely positive regarding growth projections and cost containment, with few signs of skepticism or pressing tone.
  • Management's tone was confident and optimistic in both prepared remarks and Q&A, citing "substantial tailwinds" and "no negative influences on our company that would cause earnings pressure or risk issues." There was consistent use of forward-looking statements around cost reductions and margin expansion.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's tone is more upbeat, with increased confidence stemming from resolved noise in the consumer program and a clear path to targeted profitability.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter emphasized operating leverage, scalable digital and core bank deposit growth, and the impact of cost-saving initiatives. Last quarter focused more on rebuilding earning assets and mortgage expansion, with more uncertainty around Panacea deconsolidation and consumer book volatility.
  • Guidance language this quarter is more definitive, with explicit targets like $13 million pretax pre-provision earnings and a 1% ROA by 2026.
  • Analysts' tone has shifted from cautious to more constructive, reflecting management's stronger outlook and clarity around expense and earnings trajectories.
  • Key metrics such as net interest margin, noninterest income, and expense run rates show explicit improvement from Q1.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged prior volatility from the consumer book but stated that with "the last write-off of noticeable interest on the maturing promo loans," future quarters are expected to see normalized results.
  • There is continued focus on expense containment, and management believes further cost savings are achievable through technology and vendor consolidation.
  • No new material risks were raised by analysts; management stressed there are "no negative influences on our company that would cause earnings pressure or risk issues."

Final Takeaway

Primis Financial Corp. management emphasizes that the business has transitioned to a period of stable and accelerating earnings, driven by wide operating leverage, disciplined cost control, scalable funding strategies, and targeted loan growth in core and specialty divisions. With substantial cost reductions and the consumer loan volatility largely behind, the company is positioned to achieve over $13 million in pretax pre-provision earnings and a 1% ROA by 2026, underpinned by continued momentum in core banking, mortgage, and Panacea platforms.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Predictiv AI to acquire Shift Technologies and HouseStack
data.description:
• Predictiv AI (PAI.H:CA) said on Friday it has signed a definitive agreement to acquire Shift Technologies Canada and HouseStack.
  • As per the agreement, Predictiv AI will acquire all the issued and outstanding shares of Shift and HouseStack from Suman Pushparajah and Sana Srithas, in exchange for the issuance of common shares of Predictive AI that will exist upon completion of the RTO transaction in the aggregate amount of 62.68M.
  • In connection with the closing of the RTO Transaction, Predictiv AI will consolidate its common shares on a ratio of 1 new share for each 9 old shares. Based on the number of common shares currently issued and outstanding of 128.5M, it is expected that the company will have approximately 14.28M common shares outstanding post-consolidation prior to completion of the RTO Transaction.
  • As part of the RTO Transaction, an equity financing has been completed for gross proceeds of $1,642,000 through the issuance of subscription receipts at $0.10 per Subscription Receipt, said the company.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: Universal Logistics targets $1.6B-$1.7B full-year revenue amid cost discipline and sales pipeline expansion
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Universal Logistics Holdings (ULH) Q2 2025

Management View

  • CEO Timothy Phillips opened by stating the quarter "remained a challenging environment across the transportation and logistics industry," citing muted freight markets, lower automotive production, and tough prior-year comparisons, but emphasized results were "broadly in line with our expectations."
  • Phillips noted Universal reported second quarter operating revenues of $393.8 million with net income of $8.3 million or $0.32 per diluted share, and highlighted a 5.1% operating margin and $56.2 million in EBITDA, calling these results evidence of "our continued ability to generate solid cash flows and maintain profitability in a persistently soft freight market."
  • Contract Logistics remained the company's cornerstone, with $260.6 million in revenue. Phillips stated, "The integration of Parsec continues to progress smoothly and contributed $55 million in revenue during the quarter." He noted the segment is "confident in the stability and long-term growth prospects," citing 87 value-added programs, up from 68 a year ago.
  • The Trucking segment saw revenues of $64.1 million, with Phillips commenting, "Our focus on specialized freight, including our wind energy business, continues to support more resilient margins even in a depressed market." Operating margin improved to 5.2%.
  • In Intermodal, Phillips said, "We narrowed our operating loss to $5.7 million from $10.7 million in the first quarter and sequentially improved our operating ratio 108.2 from 115.1 in Q1," reflecting progress in cost rationalization and operational optimization.
  • Phillips announced new executive leadership for enterprise-wide sales and business development, hiring of several senior sales directors, and the rollout of a new CRM solution to unify sales activities and enhance visibility into a "growing $1 billion sales pipeline."
  • CFO Jude Marcus Beres stated, "Universal Logistics Holdings reported consolidated net income of $8.3 million or $0.32 per share on total operating revenues of $393.8 million in the second quarter of 2025." Beres noted the comparison to $30.7 million net income in the same period last year, with operating income at $19.9 million and EBITDA at $56.2 million.

Outlook

  • For Q3 2025, Universal expects top line revenues between $390 million and $410 million, operating margins in the 5% to 7% range, and EBITDA margins between 14% to 16%.
  • Full-year guidance is for revenues between $1.6 billion and $1.7 billion, with operating and EBITDA margins similar to the Q3 range.
  • Capital expenditures for equipment are projected at $100 million to $125 million for the year, and real estate spending between $50 million and $65 million. Interest expense is expected in the $48 million to $51 million range.
  • The company reiterated its focus on strategic initiatives in sales, technology, and customer-centric solutions as key drivers for margin growth over the coming quarters.

Financial Results

  • Universal reported Q2 operating revenues of $393.8 million and net income of $8.3 million or $0.32 per share, with a 5.1% operating margin and EBITDA of $56.2 million.
  • Contract Logistics delivered $260.6 million in revenue and $21.8 million in operating income with an 8.4% margin. The Parsec acquisition contributed $55 million in revenue.
  • Intermodal revenues were $68.9 million, with an operating loss of $5.7 million and an operating ratio of 108.2.
  • Trucking revenues were $64.1 million, with $3.3 million in operating income and a 5.2% margin. The segment saw a 22.6% drop in load volumes and an 8.9% decrease in revenue per load.
  • Cash and equivalents at quarter end were $24.3 million, with $795.5 million in net interest-bearing debt. Capital expenditures were $84.3 million.
  • The board declared a $0.105 per share dividend, payable October 1, 2025.

Q&A

  • Andrew Baxter Baxter Cox, Stifel: Asked about the impact of tariffs and customer restocking. Phillips responded that tariffs "did have an impact on our Intermodal division and imports," particularly with discount retailers sourcing from China, but saw lesser impact on other divisions. He added, "We believe we will see some normal cyclical uplift as we normally would in the third quarter."
  • Cox: Inquired on profitability initiatives for Intermodal and the timeline to turn the segment around. Phillips said, "Our goal is to return to profitability in the third or fourth quarter," emphasizing new sales leadership, cross-selling, and ongoing cost rationalization.
  • Cox: Asked about the outlook for OEM and Class 8 truck production, given declines. Beres responded, "There’s not a lot of positive outlook for that space," citing import tariffs and a soft trucking backdrop as headwinds.
  • Cox: Questioned growth prospects for the Trucking segment. Beres explained that the wind franchise is expected to recover in the second half, and Phillips noted, "We’re pretty bullish on the specialized and will continue to focus on building that product out."
  • Cox: Follow-up on the "Big Beautiful Bill" and incentives for wind projects. Phillips said, "We expect '27, '28, '29 to be pretty fruitful years based on the fact that those projects will be in full swing..."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts' tone was neutral to slightly skeptical, focusing on tariff impacts, profitability timelines, and segment-level performance.
  • Management maintained a confident yet cautious tone in prepared remarks, with Phillips stating, "I am confident in our team, our strategy and our ability to adapt and execute," but acknowledged the challenging macro environment.
  • During Q&A, management provided detailed responses, showing transparency regarding headwinds but emphasizing ongoing initiatives for improvement.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both management and analysts exhibited slightly more optimism due to sequential improvements, but the tone remains measured.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Q2 revenues and earnings improved slightly from Q1, with net income rising from $6 million to $8.3 million and operating margins from 4.1% to 5.1%.
  • Contract Logistics revenue and operating income were up from Q1, while Intermodal narrowed its operating loss from $10.7 million in Q1 to $5.7 million in Q2.
  • Trucking continued to face volume pressure but saw improved margins and operating income compared to Q1.
  • Management's tone shifted from defensive in Q1 to more confident in Q2, citing ongoing cost discipline, sales expansion, and operational improvements.
  • Analysts' focus remained on segment-level improvements and macroeconomic headwinds, but the discussion turned more toward pathways to recovery and execution of turnaround plans.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited ongoing softness in freight markets, lower automotive production, and persistent pressure on Intermodal as key challenges.
  • Tariff impacts and uncertainty around OEM and Class 8 truck production were highlighted as significant headwinds.
  • The company is "doubling down on strategic initiatives to strengthen our sales engine" and continuing to rationalize costs and optimize operations to counteract market headwinds.
  • Analysts raised concerns about the timeline to profitability for Intermodal and the sustainability of Trucking segment improvements, which management addressed with detailed plans for sales and operational changes.

Final Takeaway

Universal Logistics Holdings navigated a tough second quarter with disciplined cost control, strategic sales expansion, and ongoing integration of key acquisitions. Management remains confident in its ability to drive long-term growth, supported by a robust sales pipeline, enhanced commercial capabilities, and a diversified service portfolio. While macro challenges persist, actions taken over recent quarters are positioning the company for margin improvement and revenue growth, with full-year guidance reaffirmed and clear operational targets set for the remainder of 2025.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: First Financial Bancorp outlines low to mid-single digit loan growth and stable credit costs for Q3 2025 amid record revenue
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: First Financial Bancorp. (FFBC) Q2 2025

Management View

  • Archie M. Brown, President and CEO, announced record revenue of $226.3 million for the second quarter, a 5% increase from the prior year, and highlighted adjusted earnings per share of $0.74, return on assets of 1.54%, and return on tangible common equity of 20%. Brown noted that "the company's industry-leading profitability was once again driven by a robust net interest margin." He pointed out broad-based loan growth except for commercial real estate, which declined due to higher payoffs. Brown stated, "Q3 scheduled maturities in the IC portfolio are lower, and we expect higher overall loan growth in the second half of this year." He also announced that the Board of Directors approved a $0.01 increase in the common dividend to $0.25 per share.
  • James Michael Anderson, CFO & COO, reported, "The second quarter results were excellent and included strong earnings, record revenues driven by a robust net interest margin, solid loan and deposit growth and declining net charge-offs." He emphasized that "net interest margin remains very strong at 4.05%, which represented a 17 basis point increase from the first quarter." Anderson highlighted that average deposit balances increased by $114 million and pointed to "double-digit percentage growth in mortgage and bankcard income." He also stated, "Tangible book value increased $0.60 to $15.40, while our tangible common equity ratio increased 24 basis points to 8.4%."

Outlook

  • Brown stated, "Over the second half of the year, we expect easing payoff pressures combined with higher production to accelerate our growth." He projected, "Specific to the third quarter, we expect loan growth to be in the low to mid-single digits on an annualized basis." He further articulated that "core deposit balances are expected to be stable over the next quarter, excluding seasonal deposit outflows." Brown said, "We expect our net interest margin...to be in the range between 4% and 4.05% over the next quarter, assuming a 25 basis point rate cut in September." He also noted expectations for credit costs to "approximate prior-quarter levels and charge-offs to be in the 20 to 25 basis point range for the third quarter, while ACL coverage as a percentage of loans is expected to be stable to slightly increasing."
  • Management expects fee income to be between $67 million and $69 million and noninterest expense to be between $128 million and $130 million.
  • Brown mentioned the acquisition of Westfield Bank, stating, "Appropriate applications have been filed with our regulators, and we continue to expect approval and closing to occur this year."

Financial Results

  • The company reported record revenue of $226.3 million, adjusted earnings per share of $0.74, adjusted noninterest income of $67.8 million, and a net interest margin of 4.05%. Loan growth was 2% on an annualized basis, and average deposit balances increased by $114 million.
  • Adjusted net income for the quarter was $70.6 million, and tangible book value per share increased to $15.40. The tangible common equity ratio increased to 8.4%.
  • Net charge-offs declined to 21 basis points, and asset quality remained stable with classified asset balances at 1.15% of total assets. The allowance for credit losses (ACL) coverage increased slightly to 1.34% of total loans.
  • The Board approved a $0.01 increase in the common dividend to $0.25 per share, maintaining a payout ratio of approximately 35% of net income.

Q&A

  • Daniel Tamayo, Raymond James: Asked about the sustainability of lower deposit costs and the outlook for funding costs. Anderson replied, "We're pretty close to that at this point...with rates stabilizing here, I think...you'll see that 12 or 13 basis point drop in our deposit costs, maybe a little bit more than the peer group, and that's that lag that I was referring to."
  • Terence James McEvoy, Stephens Inc.: Inquired about efficiency initiatives and cost reduction. Brown responded, "We really are going through the whole bank...literally looking at every function, every department...probably 80% of the way through the company at this point...there's some technology in a couple of these areas we're implementing in the back half of this year."
  • David Konrad, KBW: Questioned asset quality and the increase in C&I nonaccruals. William R. Harrod explained, "Our quarter-over-quarter increase in the NPAs is driven by downgrades of 2 commercial borrowers, one of which was significantly impacted by the tariffs...The other relationship is a contract manufacturer, which is currently going through a sale process."
  • Karl Robert Shepard, RBC Capital Markets: Asked about loan growth in business lines and margin timing. Anderson stated, "In the other business lines, we're seeing consistent growth...our specialty lines will grow maybe in that 10% to 12% range."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts focused primarily on margin sustainability, loan growth, and asset quality, with questions reflecting a moderately positive tone and interest in future performance.
  • Management maintained a confident and optimistic tone, emphasizing stability and growth, as seen in Brown's statement, "We're very pleased with our second quarter and year-to-date financial performance, and we remain very excited about our outlook for the remainder of 2025 and beyond."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both management and analysts exhibited increased confidence, with management's tone shifting from cautious optimism to strong affirmation of performance and outlook.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for loan growth increased from low single digits to low to mid-single digits for the third quarter, with management expressing higher confidence in easing payoff pressures and improved loan production.
  • Net interest margin guidance moved from 3.95%-4.05% in Q1 to 4%-4.05% in Q2, with margin expansion achieved this quarter.
  • Noninterest income and expenses both increased modestly, reflecting continued growth in core businesses and investment in efficiency initiatives.
  • Analysts shifted focus from concerns about tariff impacts and asset sensitivity in Q1 to margin sustainability and growth strategies in Q2.
  • Management's tone moved from highlighting caution and external risks in Q1 to emphasizing robust profitability and strategic execution in Q2.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management highlighted commercial real estate payoffs as a headwind to loan growth but expects these pressures to subside in the second half of the year.
  • Asset quality risks are being managed, with net charge-offs declining and classified assets stable. Harrod referenced specific C&I borrowers affected by tariffs and business transitions but indicated resolutions are expected by year-end.
  • Management continues to monitor deposit concentrations and uninsured deposits, noting that borrowing capacity provides flexibility in case of stress events.

Final Takeaway

First Financial Bancorp. management highlighted a quarter of record revenue and robust profitability, supported by strong net interest margin expansion, disciplined expense management, and stable asset quality. Management expressed confidence in accelerating loan growth and maintaining industry-leading margins in the second half of 2025, while continuing to invest in efficiency and integration of the Westfield Bank acquisition. The company signaled its ongoing commitment to shareholder returns with an increased dividend and positive outlook for the remainder of the year.
